_note: Conditionals. Curriculum sequence follows Foundations of Python Programming (Runestone) ch.6 and Think Python ch.5. Both are copyleft (GFDL / CC BY-NC-SA), so NO content is copied from either: only the topic order is used, and a topic sequence is not copyrightable. Every question below is original. Concepts covered: if/else, the elif-else-if chain stopping at the first true branch, order of branches, == vs =, boolean operators, and the body being decided by indentation or braces.

python.explanation: 15 is not greater than or equal to 18, so the condition is false and the `else` branch runs. Exactly one of the two branches runs, never both.

java.explanation: 15 is not greater than or equal to 18, so the condition is false and the `else` branch runs. Exactly one of the two branches runs, never both.

javascript.explanation: 15 is not greater than or equal to 18, so the condition is false and the `else` branch runs. Exactly one of the two branches runs, never both.

cpp.explanation: 15 is not greater than or equal to 18, so the condition is false and the `else` branch runs. Exactly one of the two branches runs, never both.

questions.title: A chain stops at the first true branch

python.explanation: 95 is greater than or equal to 80, so the first branch wins and the chain stops right there. The `elif` is never even checked, so the A is unreachable for every score. This is why order matters: put the strictest condition first, or every A student in your class quietly gets a B.

java.explanation: 95 is greater than or equal to 80, so the first branch wins and the chain stops. The `else if` is never checked, so the A is unreachable for every score. Order matters: put the strictest condition first, or every A student quietly gets a B.

javascript.explanation: 95 is greater than or equal to 80, so the first branch wins and the chain stops. The `else if` is never checked, so the A is unreachable for every score. Order matters: put the strictest condition first, or every A student quietly gets a B.

cpp.explanation: 95 is greater than or equal to 80, so the first branch wins and the chain stops. The `else if` is never checked, so the A is unreachable for every score. Order matters: put the strictest condition first, or every A student quietly gets a B.

questions.title: Two ifs are not a chain

python.explanation: These are two separate `if` statements, not a chain, so both conditions get checked and both are true. Both lines print. Swap the second `if` for an `elif` and only the first would run. That one word decides whether your conditions are alternatives or independent questions.

java.explanation: These are two separate `if` statements, not a chain, so both conditions get checked and both are true. Both lines print. Write `else if` instead and only the first would run. That one word decides whether your conditions are alternatives or independent questions.

javascript.explanation: These are two separate `if` statements, not a chain, so both conditions get checked and both are true. Both lines print. Write `else if` instead and only the first would run. That one word decides whether your conditions are alternatives or independent questions.

cpp.explanation: These are two separate `if` statements, not a chain, so both conditions get checked and both are true. Both lines print. Write `else if` instead and only the first would run. That one word decides whether your conditions are alternatives or independent questions.

questions.title: One equals sign inside an if

choices: SyntaxError, the file does not run at all
It prints nothing and sets x to 5
It prints nothing, because x is 3
python.explanation: A single `=` assigns a value; a double `==` asks whether two values match. Inside a condition you are always asking, so Python rejects the file before running a single line. Read the line out loud: if the words are "is x five", you want two.

java.prompt: What happens when you compile this?
choices: Compile error: incompatible types, int cannot be converted to boolean
It prints nothing and sets x to 5
It prints nothing, because x is 3
java.explanation: `x = 5` assigns and produces the int 5, but an `if` needs a boolean. Java refuses to compile it, which is a friendly outcome: the mistake cannot hide. In C and C++ this same line compiles and is always true, which is far more dangerous.

choices: It prints five, because the assignment produces 5, which is truthy
It prints nothing, because x is 3
javascript.explanation: This is the dangerous case. `x = 5` assigns 5 to x, and the whole expression produces 5. Since 5 is truthy, the branch runs and it prints "five". No error, no warning, and the condition is now true no matter what x was. Use `===` when you mean to compare.

choices: It prints five, because the assignment produces 5, which is truthy
It prints nothing, because x is 3
cpp.explanation: This is the most dangerous typo in C++. `x = 5` assigns and produces 5, and any non-zero value counts as true, so the branch always runs. It compiles cleanly and gives no warning by default. Java rejects this line; C++ happily runs it.

questions.title: and needs both, or needs one

python.explanation: `and` requires both sides to be true. The age passes, but `has_id` is False, so the whole condition is false and the `else` runs. Change `and` to `or` and it would print allowed, since one true side would be enough.

java.explanation: `&&` requires both sides to be true. The age passes, but `hasId` is false, so the whole condition is false and the `else` runs. Change `&&` to `||` and it would print allowed, since one true side would be enough.

javascript.explanation: `&&` requires both sides to be true. The age passes, but `hasId` is false, so the whole condition is false and the `else` runs. Change `&&` to `||` and it would print allowed, since one true side would be enough.

cpp.explanation: `&&` requires both sides to be true. The age passes, but `hasId` is false, so the whole condition is false and the `else` runs. Change `&&` to `||` and it would print allowed, since one true side would be enough.

questions.title: Which line belongs to the if?

python.code:
score = 50
if score >= 80:
    print("nice work")
print("done")
python.explanation: Only the indented line is inside the `if`. `print("done")` is back at the left margin, so it is not part of the branch and runs for any score at all. The indentation is what decides membership, so a line that looks close to the `if` is not necessarily inside it.

java.explanation: With no braces, an `if` takes only the single statement after it. The second `println` is indented to look like it belongs, but Java ignores indentation, so it sits outside the `if` and always runs. This is exactly why you should always write the braces, even for a single line.

javascript.explanation: With no braces, an `if` takes only the single statement after it. The second `console.log` is indented to look like it belongs, but JavaScript ignores indentation, so it sits outside the `if` and always runs. This is why you should always write the braces.

cpp.explanation: With no braces, an `if` takes only the single statement after it. The second `cout` is indented to look like it belongs, but C++ ignores indentation, so it sits outside the `if` and always runs. This is why you should always write the braces.

python.explanation: Check the branches in order. 85 is not at least 90, so A is skipped. 85 is at least 80, so `grade` becomes B and the chain stops. The later branches are never even looked at. This chain is written correctly: strictest first, so each branch only has to rule out what came before it.

java.explanation: Check the branches in order. 85 is not at least 90, so A is skipped. 85 is at least 80, so `grade` becomes B and the chain stops. The later branches are never looked at. This chain is written correctly: strictest first, so each branch only has to rule out what came before it.

javascript.explanation: Check the branches in order. 85 is not at least 90, so A is skipped. 85 is at least 80, so `grade` becomes B and the chain stops. The later branches are never looked at. This chain is written correctly: strictest first, so each branch only has to rule out what came before it.

cpp.explanation: Check the branches in order. 85 is not at least 90, so A is skipped. 85 is at least 80, so `grade` becomes B and the chain stops. The later branches are never looked at. This chain is written correctly: strictest first, so each branch only has to rule out what came before it.

python.explanation: `not` flips a boolean. `is_open` is False, so `not is_open` is True and the first branch runs. Reading it aloud helps: "if not open, print closed".

java.explanation: `!` flips a boolean. `isOpen` is false, so `!isOpen` is true and the first branch runs. Reading it aloud helps: "if not open, print closed".

javascript.explanation: `!` flips a boolean. `isOpen` is false, so `!isOpen` is true and the first branch runs. Reading it aloud helps: "if not open, print closed".

cpp.explanation: `!` flips a boolean. `isOpen` is false, so `!isOpen` is true and the first branch runs. Reading it aloud helps: "if not open, print closed".

python.explanation: The outer `if` passes, so we go inside it. There, `is_admin` is False, so the inner `else` runs. The outer `else` is only reached when `logged_in` is false, so it never comes into play here. Nesting reads like a decision tree: answer the first question, then the next.

java.explanation: The outer `if` passes, so we go inside it. There, `isAdmin` is false, so the inner `else` runs. The outer `else` is only reached when `loggedIn` is false, so it never comes into play here. Nesting reads like a decision tree: answer the first question, then the next.

javascript.explanation: The outer `if` passes, so we go inside it. There, `isAdmin` is false, so the inner `else` runs. The outer `else` is only reached when `loggedIn` is false, so it never comes into play here. Nesting reads like a decision tree: answer the first question, then the next.

cpp.explanation: The outer `if` passes, so we go inside it. There, `isAdmin` is false, so the inner `else` runs. The outer `else` is only reached when `loggedIn` is false, so it never comes into play here. Nesting reads like a decision tree: answer the first question, then the next.

python.explanation: `>` means strictly greater than, and 80 is not greater than 80, so the `else` runs. If a score of exactly 80 was meant to pass, the condition should be `>=`. Boundary values are where conditions go wrong, so test the exact edge every time.

java.explanation: `>` means strictly greater than, and 80 is not greater than 80, so the `else` runs. If a score of exactly 80 was meant to pass, the condition should be `>=`. Boundary values are where conditions go wrong, so test the exact edge every time.

javascript.explanation: `>` means strictly greater than, and 80 is not greater than 80, so the `else` runs. If a score of exactly 80 was meant to pass, the condition should be `>=`. Boundary values are where conditions go wrong, so test the exact edge every time.

cpp.explanation: `>` means strictly greater than, and 80 is not greater than 80, so the `else` runs. If a score of exactly 80 was meant to pass, the condition should be `>=`. Boundary values are where conditions go wrong, so test the exact edge every time.
